competitors.name: NetNewsWire
competitors.model: Native desktop (macOS) + iOS; MIT open source; free, donation/volunteer-driven
competitors.strengths: Gold standard for free native Apple RSS; fast, polished, mature (v7 in 2026); syncs as a CLIENT to Feedbin/Feedly/Inoreader/iCloud/GReader-API servers — the proof that 'desktop app + pluggable sync backends' is a viable OSS architecture; huge trust/brand in the RSS community
competitors.weaknesses: Apple-only (no Windows/Linux); iCloud sync is notoriously slow/broken (multi-hour initial syncs, state-sync failures tracked in issues #3901/#4024); plain reading experience — no rich per-platform views, no read-later pipeline, no export-to-notes story, minimal filtering/rules

competitors.name: Miniflux
competitors.model: Self-hosted web server (Go); Apache-2.0; free OSS, paid hosted option ($15/yr)
competitors.strengths: Minimalist, extremely fast, low-memory, rock-solid; exposes Fever + Google Reader APIs so native mobile clients (Reeder Classic, Capy Reader, FeedMe, NewsFlash) attach cleanly; excellent docs; the default answer for 'self-hosted RSS' among engineers
competitors.weaknesses: Requires PostgreSQL (no SQLite), so not zero-ops; single maintainer; deliberately feature-sparse UI — no rich media views, weak read-later, no notes/export pipeline; server ops burden is exactly what local-first users don't want

competitors.name: FreshRSS
competitors.model: Self-hosted web server (PHP); AGPL-3.0; free OSS
competitors.strengths: Large community, extensions/themes, multi-user, SQLite-or-Postgres, scales to many feeds; best-in-class GReader-API server implementation — the de facto sync hub the OSS mobile-client ecosystem builds against; web scraping/XPath for full-content
competitors.weaknesses: Dated PHP web UI; power features live in extensions of varying quality; still a server to run; reading experience (typography, media, read-later) far behind Readwise/Reeder; no markdown/notes export

competitors.name: Feedbin
competitors.model: Hosted web service + source on GitHub (MIT, Rails) but hostile to self-hosting in practice; $5/mo single tier
competitors.strengths: Beloved simple pricing, clean UI; best-known newsletter-to-feed email address feature; strong API that many native clients (NetNewsWire, Unread, Reeder Classic) sync against; podcast/YouTube feed support
competitors.weaknesses: Cloud dependency — your archive lives on their servers; self-hosting is documented-but-painful (multi-service Rails stack); no offline desktop app; no vault/notes export beyond basic integrations

competitors.name: Inoreader
competitors.model: Hosted web + mobile apps; proprietary; freemium, Pro ~$90/yr, ad-supported free tier
competitors.strengths: The power-user feature ceiling: rules/filters/pipelines, dedup, search operators, newsletter ingestion, annotations, offline mobile, 150-feed free tier; widely recommended 'best for power users' in 2026 roundups
competitors.weaknesses: Feature-gating creep — 'most worthwhile features gated behind the most expensive plan'; ads on free; proprietary silo with data on their servers; heavy/cluttered UI; privacy posture unattractive to the self-host crowd

competitors.name: Readwise Reader
competitors.model: Hosted web + mobile + desktop wrappers; proprietary; $9.99/mo annual ($120/yr)
competitors.strengths: The benchmark for read-it-later + PKM: RSS + newsletters + PDFs + EPUBs + YouTube-with-transcript in one inbox, highlights, Ghostreader AI, spaced-repetition resurfacing, and the category-best OFFICIAL Obsidian export plugin (Jinja2-templated markdown, auto-sync) — it proved 'reading → markdown vault' is a killer workflow
competitors.weaknesses: Expensive for casual users, and price is the #1 complaint; entirely cloud-hosted (your library dies with the company — the exact fear Omnivore/Pocket validated); AI credits exhaust; closed source; no local-first mode

competitors.name: Folo (follow.is, RSSNext)
competitors.model: Desktop (Electron) + mobile + web; client AGPL-3.0 (with icon-license exception) but backend is a proprietary hosted service requiring an account; VC/crypto-funded (RSS3 ecosystem, $POWER token economy)
competitors.strengths: 36k+ GitHub stars — proof of massive demand for a modern 'everything-is-a-feed' reader; beautiful UI; per-media view modes (articles/social/pictures/video/audio) — the closest existing product to Curio's rich-views concept; AI timeline summaries/digests; MCP server for agents
competitors.weaknesses: 'Open source' client, closed backend — unusable without their cloud; crypto token incentives and tracker reports poison trust with exactly the privacy/self-host audience; Electron heft; data not locally owned; longevity tied to token economics

competitors.name: Omnivore (dead) + forks
competitors.model: Was: hosted + AGPL-3.0 OSS read-later; acquihired by ElevenLabs Nov 2024, service killed with ~2 weeks notice, user data deleted
competitors.strengths: Had the best free OSS read-later UX, labels, highlights, newsletters, and a popular Obsidian sync plugin; its death is the market-shaping event — thousands of displaced users migrated to Karakeep/Readeck/Wallabag/Readwise and now select for 'can't be taken away from me'
competitors.weaknesses: Self-hosting the corpse is miserable (PostgreSQL + Elasticsearch + microservices); community fork (omnivore.work) exists but momentum scattered; lesson for Curio: VC-free governance, boring deps, data-on-disk, and an exit-friendly export are FEATURES to advertise

competitors.name: Karakeep (ex-Hoarder)
competitors.model: Self-hosted web server (Docker, Next.js) + mobile apps + browser extension; AGPL-3.0; free OSS + new paid cloud beta
competitors.strengths: Fastest-growing Omnivore/Pocket successor (~25k stars); bookmark-everything (links/notes/images/PDFs); AI auto-tagging via OpenAI OR local Ollama — the template for optional-BYO-AI done right; full-text search; one-click capture
competitors.weaknesses: It's a bookmark pile, not a reader — no feed triage workflow, weak RSS; archives open live URL by default; logged-in pages archive poorly; Docker setup intimidates non-technical users; no markdown-vault export contract

competitors.name: Readeck
competitors.model: Self-hosted web server (single Go binary + SQLite); AGPL-3.0; free OSS
competitors.strengths: Simplicity darling of 2025-26 migrations: one binary, SQLite, fast on a Pi; highlights, e-book (EPUB/Kobo) export, in-app archival incl. video transcripts; KOReader plugin emerging — the e-ink angle
competitors.weaknesses: Small community (~1k stars vs Wallabag's 12.6k); thin third-party ecosystem and API surface; no RSS aggregation (read-later only); no notes/vault pipeline

competitors.name: Wallabag
competitors.model: Self-hosted web (PHP/Symfony) + mobile apps; MIT; free OSS + wallabag.it hosted (~€11/yr)
competitors.strengths: 13 years old, most-integrated self-hosted read-later: browser extensions, mobile apps, KOReader/e-ink plugins, RSS-reader hooks; annotation support; the safe default Omnivore replacement
competitors.weaknesses: Aging UX; parser quality trails Readability-class extractors; heavier PHP stack; users increasingly defect to Readeck/Karakeep for speed and polish; no markdown-vault export, no feed reading

competitors.name: Reeder (new) / Reeder Classic
competitors.model: Native Apple-only (macOS/iOS); proprietary; new Reeder is subscription, Classic is one-time
competitors.strengths: New Reeder is the mainstream 'unified timeline' experiment: RSS + YouTube + Reddit + Mastodon + Bluesky + podcasts + comics in one app with iCloud position sync — validates Curio's multi-platform-views bet; gorgeous design; Classic remains the best GReader/Fever/Miniflux client on Apple
competitors.weaknesses: New version removed unread counts and third-party sync (iCloud only), alienating power users ('feels like a social app, not a tool'); no feed folders; Apple-only; closed source; the backlash is a published map of what power users refuse to give up: unread counts, folders, sync-service choice

competitors.name: Unread
competitors.strengths: Best gesture-driven reading aesthetic on iOS; syncs with Feedbin/Feedly/Fever/Inoreader/NewsBlur + own Unread Cloud; webpage-text pre-caching; special Reddit link-post handling (2026)
competitors.weaknesses: Reading-only client, not an aggregation brain; Apple-only; subscription for basics like caching; small feature surface for 300+ feed triage

competitors.name: Feedly
competitors.model: Hosted web + mobile; proprietary; freemium, Pro+ $99/yr, pivoted to enterprise threat-intel
competitors.strengths: Smoothest onboarding, biggest brand, 100-source free tier, AI 'Leo' filtering; enterprise revenue keeps it alive
competitors.weaknesses: 2026 consensus: optimizing for enterprise intelligence, not readers; search paywalled; power features expensive; notorious for tracking/ads posture; individual power users are actively migrating off — a harvestable user base

competitors.name: NewsBlur
competitors.model: Hosted + fully self-hostable (Docker, whole stack incl. mobile sync); MIT; $36/yr hosted, free tier
competitors.strengths: Only major service that is MIT-licensed end-to-end incl. iOS/Android apps; 'intelligence training' (per-feed like/dislike classifiers) is the original local ranking model; original-site view; 15 years of survival without VC
competitors.weaknesses: Dated UI; heavy multi-service self-host stack (MongoDB, Postgres, Redis, Elasticsearch); training model feels ancient next to LLM-era expectations; niche mindshare

competitors.name: OSS desktop field: Fluent Reader / Lettura / NewsFlash / papr
competitors.model: Fluent Reader: Electron, BSD-3, effectively stale; Lettura: Tauri, free OSS, low-activity solo project; NewsFlash: GTK/Rust for Linux, GPL, active GReader/Miniflux/FreshRSS client; papr (2026): native + agent-facing CLI for AI-agent feed triage, OSS
competitors.strengths: Collectively prove the demand for a cross-platform desktop OSS reader and that Tauri works for it; NewsFlash shows client-to-self-hosted-server is the winning sync pattern on Linux; papr shows the emerging 'agent-readable feeds' niche (CLI/MCP)
competitors.weaknesses: This is the graveyard Curio must not join: solo-maintainer stalls, shallow feature sets, no read-later pipeline, no export story, no mobile answer — there is NO healthy, actively-maintained, power-user, cross-platform OSS desktop reader in 2026. The seat is empty

competitors.name: OSS mobile field: Capy Reader / ReadYou (Android), + Slax Reader (web)
competitors.model: Capy Reader: Android, OSS, free, syncs to Feedbin/FreshRSS/Miniflux via GReader API (active, June 2026 releases); ReadYou: Android Material You, OSS, local-focused; Slax Reader: Apache-2.0 web read-later, 'Omnivore but alive', AI outlines
competitors.strengths: A healthy OSS mobile-client ecosystem already exists AGAINST the GReader API — Curio doesn't have to build mobile to have a mobile story, it has to speak the protocol
competitors.weaknesses: None of them are Curio competitors head-on; they are complements. Slax is the closest new read-later competitor but is web/cloud-first

table_stakes: OPML import/export with folder structure preserved, round-trip safe; feed autodiscovery from a bare URL; favicon fetch — first-run must ingest a 500-feed OPML from Feedly/Inoreader/FreshRSS without loss
Full-text extraction for truncated feeds (Readability-class) with HTML sanitization, plus 'original site' fallback view — users assume one keystroke gets the whole article

Fast keyboard-first triage: j/k navigation, mark-read semantics (item/feed/folder/all-older-than), unread counts (the new-Reeder backlash proves removing them is disqualifying), saved filters/smart views
Full-text search over the entire local archive (SQLite FTS5 tier), instant at 100k+ items; performance at 300–1000 feeds is assumed, not a feature
Read-later queue distinct from stars, plus tags/labels; read/unread/star state that never gets lost
Filters and rules: keyword mute, auto-mark-read, auto-tag, per-feed refresh intervals — high-volume users cannot function without noise controls (Inoreader set the bar)

Sync/mobile answer — the #1 churn driver for desktop-only readers: in 2026 users assume read-state follows them to phone and couch; minimum credible answer is first-class GReader-API + Fever + Miniflux-API CLIENT mode so an existing FreshRSS/Miniflux/Feedbin account is the sync brain and Capy Reader/Reeder Classic/Unread cover mobile; standalone-local mode remains for no-server users
Offline-complete reading: all article text and images cached locally; the app must be fully usable on a plane
Polite fetching: conditional GET (ETag/Last-Modified), per-host rate limits, gzip, good User-Agent, dead-feed and permanent-redirect handling with a feed-health surface
Newsletter ingestion path (email-to-feed) — Feedbin/Inoreader/Readwise made this assumed for the read-later audience; even a v2 IMAP-pull answer must be on the roadmap
Podcast enclosures handled at least as playable/downloadable items; YouTube channel feeds render watchably

Total data portability advertised loudly: one-command export of everything (OPML + articles + state), documented schema — post-Omnivore/post-Pocket, 'you can leave anytime' is emotionally table stakes for this audience
Dark mode, typography/measure controls, and a settings surface that respects power users; system-native feel on macOS/Windows/Linux
Browser extension or share/protocol handler to subscribe-to-this-page and save-this-URL from the browser
Import from the dead and dying: Pocket export files, Omnivore archives, Instapaper, Readwise CSV — switching-cost killers that every successful successor (Karakeep, Readeck) shipped
differentiation: The empty seat: there is no healthy, actively maintained, cross-platform, power-user OSS desktop reader in 2026 — NetNewsWire is Apple-only, Fluent Reader is stale Electron, Lettura is a low-activity solo Tauri project, NewsFlash is Linux/GTK-niche. A polished Tauri app on macOS+Windows+Linux with a 1000-feed triage workflow wins this category by showing up and staying maintained

Markdown-vault export as a first-class product contract, not a plugin: deterministic CommonMark + YAML frontmatter, user-templated, incremental, to any folder (Obsidian/Logseq/plain git repo). The proprietary benchmark is Readwise's $120/yr Obsidian pipeline; the OSS field has only toy Obsidian plugins. 'Your reading becomes your notes, on your disk, in a format that outlives this app' is simultaneously the Knowledge-Plane contract and the strongest post-Omnivore marketing line in the space

Reading-behavior event log as an open data product: append-only JSONL of read/star/read-later/dwell events with a documented schema. No competitor exposes reading behavior as user-owned data; it powers PKM pipelines, interest models, and agent workflows, and it's near-free to build on top of the existing SQLite layer

Per-platform rich views without accounts or API keys: Reddit thread view (public JSON), YouTube grid + player + yt-dlp metadata, podcast player/queue/downloads. Only new-Reeder (proprietary, Apple-only, dumbed-down sync) and Folo (closed backend, crypto-tainted) attempt this; an OSS, local, no-account version of 'everything is a feed' is an open niche with proven demand (Folo's 36k stars)

Trust positioning the incumbents can't copy: local-first, no account, no server required, no telemetry, SQLite file you can open yourself, MIT/Apache-class licensing, boring dependencies. Post-Omnivore/Pocket/Feedly-enterprise, 'can't be enshittified, can't be acquihired away' is a rational purchasing criterion — make longevity architecture (data on disk, exportable everything) the headline

Sync pragmatism instead of sync denial: ship GReader/Fever/Miniflux CLIENT mode so Curio is the desktop cockpit over the user's existing FreshRSS/Miniflux/Feedbin, inheriting their mobile apps — honest framing: Curio doesn't solve mobile in v1, it federates into the ecosystem that already did. Optionally later: file-based state sync (CRDT journal over Syncthing/iCloud Drive/NFS) for the serverless crowd, and vault-promotion-as-sync for the KP pattern

Rust core as a reusable library + headless CLI: the fetch/normalize/extract/export pipeline as a crate powering the GUI, a CLI (cron-able fetch/export), and an MCP server — riding the 2026 'agent-readable feeds' wave (papr, Folo MCP) that no established reader serves in an open, local way

Performance as a feature: Tauri + Rust + SQLite FTS5 can make 100k-item search and cold-start instant where Electron (Folo, Fluent Reader) and web UIs (FreshRSS) visibly lag — benchmark it publicly at 1000 feeds
above_beyond_ideas: Vault export engine (the flagship): per-feed/per-tag routing rules, Jinja-style templates for frontmatter+body, stable dedup keys/slugs, incremental sync with a manifest, archived images copied as vault-relative assets, one-keystroke 'promote to vault' in the reader, optional auto-git-commit — generic 'markdown vault' integration that any Obsidian/Logseq/git user configures, and the Knowledge Plane consumes unmodified

Open events stream: JSONL event log (subscribed/read/starred/read-later/promoted/highlighted/dwell-seconds, article URL+ids+ts) with a versioned published schema, plus `curio events tail` — the raw material for interest models, quantified-self dashboards, and spaced-resurfacing tools

curio-core crate + `curio` CLI + MCP server: headless fetch/query/export against the same SQLite; agents can triage feeds, search the archive, and pull markdown — the first power reader designed to be driven by both a human and their agent

Sync-service client mode at launch: log into FreshRSS/Miniflux/Feedbin/Inoreader via GReader/Fever/native APIs, two-way read/star state — instantly gives Curio users mobile (Capy Reader, Reeder Classic, Unread) and kills the #1 switching objection

Local rules + trainable ranking: keyword/regex/author filters, auto-tag, and a NewsBlur-style like/dislike per-feed scorer running locally (simple classifier, no cloud) that sorts the triage view by predicted interest; expose the model's features so it's inspectable

Strictly-optional BYO-AI (Karakeep pattern): point at Ollama or any OpenAI-compatible endpoint for summaries, auto-tagging, daily digest note exported into the vault; zero AI calls unless configured, works fully without it

Rich views v1: Reddit card feed + full comment threads (public JSON, no auth), YouTube channel grid + embedded player + metadata/transcripts via bundled yt-dlp (no API key), podcast player with queue/downloads/position-tracking (positions land in the event log); v2: Mastodon/Bluesky public feeds, comics mode

Link-rot-proof archive: content-addressed local snapshot (sanitized readable HTML + images) for starred/read-later items, so saved articles survive site death; 'archive view' is the default for saved items (fixing Karakeep's top complaint)
Local-first newsletter ingestion: poll an IMAP folder / plus-address in the user's own mailbox and convert emails to feed items — nobody offers newsletter-to-feed without a cloud middleman
E-reader lane: batch the read-later queue to EPUB / send-to-Kobo (Readeck precedent) — cheap feature, disproportionate loyalty from the e-ink crowd

Switcher's toolkit: importers for Pocket exports, Omnivore archives, Instapaper CSV, Readwise CSV, Feedly/Inoreader OPML+starred, plus a migration doc per source — with 'export everything back out' as the first documented command (`curio export --all`)

Highlights that live in the vault: select-to-highlight in the reader; highlights are stored as vault markdown (appended under the promoted note) rather than app-private state — the anti-Readwise: your annotations are files

Feed intelligence dashboard: dead/redirected feed detection, posting-frequency and read-ratio stats from the event log, 'you never read these 40 feeds' pruning suggestions — turns the event log into a visible product win
OPML profiles/contexts (work/research/leisure) with fast switching and per-profile vault-export targets — the 300–1000-feed power-user organizational layer no competitor has

Governance as a feature: permissive license (MIT/Apache-2.0, avoiding Folo's AGPL+proprietary-backend trust problem), public roadmap, reproducible builds, no-CLA-drama, no token, no telemetry — state in the README why this project structurally cannot repeat the Omnivore ending
